title: LLM Fundamentals
description: Transformers, tokenization, context windows, alignment, hallucinations, and model tradeoffs.

questions.title: What is a large language model and how does it generate text?
questions.scenario: An enterprise platform team runs LLM assistant used for policy search, code help, and customer-support drafting. A recent review found that the assistant sounds fluent but fails when context is missing or decoding settings are poorly controlled; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for large language model and how does it generate text measured by grounded answer rate, latency p95, token cost, and escalation rate.
questions.expectedAnswer: large language model and how does it generate text is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In LLM assistant used for policy search, code help, and customer-support drafting, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from prompts, retrieved context, token logs, safety events, and user feedback, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use grounded answer rate, latency p95, token cost, and escalation rate, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems. large language model and how does it generate text addresses that risk through model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using grounded answer rate, latency p95, token cost, and escalation rate.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from prompts, retrieved context, token logs, safety events, and user feedback. Implement the change with OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in LLM assistant used for policy search, code help, and customer-support drafting, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor grounded answer rate, latency p95, token cost, and escalation rate so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: Why is self-attention important in transformers?

commonMistakes: Explaining large language model and how does it generate text as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of grounded answer rate, latency p95, token cost, and escalation rate.
Ignoring the main risk: fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that large language model and how does it generate text improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying large language model and how does it generate text, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: An enterprise platform team applied large language model and how does it generate text in LLM assistant used for policy search, code help, and customer-support drafting. The baseline suffered from fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, then evaluated the change against grounded answer rate, latency p95, token cost, and escalation rate. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for large language model and how does it generate text, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.
